codeReview.summary: Solid implementation that covers all required behaviors. CinematicIntro component correctly implements WebGL Three.js logo reveal with custom GLSL Perlin noise dissolve shaders, Bloom/Vignette post-processing, ~5s phased animation (dark→bloom→reveal→resolve→complete), click-to-skip, sessionStorage flag, lazy-loading via React.lazy(), WebGL detection fallback, error boundary, and safety timeout. Tests are comprehensive (7 cases). LobsterIntro import fully removed from Landing.tsx. Dependencies are properly installed. Minor non-blocking issues noted below.

issues.description: The `exit={{ opacity: 0 }}` animation on the motion.div is effectively dead code. When onComplete fires, it sets both `phase='complete'` (causing the component to return null) and `introComplete=true` (causing AnimatePresence to unmount the child) in the same React batch. The component returns null before AnimatePresence can capture it for exit animation. The result is an instant disappear rather than a 0.5s fade, which is functionally fine but not the intended smooth exit.

Feature description specifies 'Framer Motion useMotionValue for sequencing' but implementation uses useState + setTimeout for phase sequencing instead. The chosen approach is simpler and works correctly — this is a reasonable implementation deviation, not a bug.

Old LobsterIntro.tsx file was not deleted. It is no longer imported anywhere (confirmed via grep) but remains as dead code in the repository. The feature description said 'Replace LobsterIntro import in Landing.tsx' which was done correctly, but cleanup of the dead file was not performed.

Potential double-call of onComplete: if the user clicks to skip at nearly the same time as the 5s phase timer fires (very tight race window), both could call onComplete before the component unmounts and clears the timers. In practice React's synchronous rendering and timer cleanup make this extremely unlikely, and handleIntroComplete (which sets sessionStorage) is idempotent, so there's no functional harm.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The old LobsterIntro.tsx component file was left in the codebase after being fully replaced. AGENTS.md doesn't mention a convention about cleaning up replaced/dead components. This could accumulate dead code over time as features are replaced.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: src/components/landing/LobsterIntro.tsx still exists (124 lines) but is imported nowhere (confirmed via grep across src/). Commit 2ae89a3 only added new files and modified Landing.tsx without deleting the old component.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker could not visually verify the WebGL animation due to headless Chromium lacking WebGL support. The skill procedure step 6 says 'Manual verification with agent-browser' but doesn't account for WebGL limitations. Worker correctly noted this in skillFeedback.deviations and the workaround (verifying fallback behavior instead) was appropriate. The user-testing.md was already updated with this limitation.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff interactiveChecks show 'WebGL is NOT available in headless browser'. skillFeedback.deviations documents the limitation. user-testing.md now includes the 'WebGL not available in headless Chromium' note under Known Limitations.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The Three.js dependencies (@react-three/fiber, @react-three/drei, @react-three/postprocessing, three) are documented in environment.md under 'New Dependencies (This Mission)'. However, @types/three is not installed and not documented. TypeScript check passes (likely because @react-three packages bundle their own Three.js type references), but this could cause issues if workers later import directly from 'three' in new files.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: package.json has three@^0.183.2 but no @types/three. `npx tsc --noEmit` passes. CinematicIntro.tsx imports `import * as THREE from 'three'` and uses THREE.Mesh, THREE.Points, THREE.BufferAttribute, THREE.ShaderMaterial types without error.

summary: PASS. The cinematic-intro-animation feature is well-implemented with comprehensive coverage of all expected behaviors: WebGL Three.js logo reveal with custom GLSL shaders, phased animation (~5s), click-to-skip, sessionStorage flag, lazy-loading, WebGL fallback, error boundary, and safety timeout. 7 unit tests cover the key paths. No blocking issues found. Non-blocking observations: dead exit animation prop, useMotionValue not used (setTimeout-based sequencing used instead, which is fine), old LobsterIntro.tsx left as dead code, and a theoretical (practically impossible) double onComplete race.
